sentences.text: We were marching-- we didn't know exactly where we were marching to.
We already on the seventh day of the march.
And we were so exhausted, that if this-- if we wouldn't much into this town, into a town-- and our guards told us we are here, keep it up, another few-- another mile or two, we are-- we are dead.
So this give us more energy to keep up this march, and to straighten out, and to show for the village, for the people of the city that we are not-- we are-- we are man.
We can walk.
We can take it.
Because we had this yellow band on our hand.
And all-- they all stopped to-- to-- to watch us.
Who are these guys coming in?
So we had this on our-- we had the feet bags and we had everything.
We had the blankets.
And we were dressed up properly against the cold, with shoes, and boots, with-- with gloves.
So we were-- we kept going now already, and we reached a place.
And they said, to halt, to stop.
We looked around.
We saw there a building of three floor-- about three, four floor building.
And there's a sign that says, the Headquarters of the Nilosh-- Nilosh party or something or-- I don't know what it was, something about the Nilosh headquarters, in Kormend.
So the city, we understood, it was called Kormend.
And it's supposed to be a city which here, they will-- they will take us from here to Germany, or hand us over to the Germans, something like that.
So we are waiting in line there, three, three, three.
And the first three guys were supposed to get inside, get registered, and pass a medical check up.
That's what the officer told us, walking in with all-- everything he has on.
So we are waiting for these three guys to come out.
It took about 20 minutes.
And these-- when-- when three-- these three characters came out, we didn't recognize them.
They were so badly beaten.
And they were dressed in-- in such a dirty and-- and-- and torn [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pants.
And he had a jacket without-- without a shirt, shoes without laces.
And the other guy couldn't even put on the shoes.
The shoes were smaller with-- with one size.
He couldn't put them on.
He had to come barefoot.
And he was bleeding from the nose from-- the eyes were swollen.
And he hardly could walk and talk.
As they came to us, and we were going to see what happened, they dropped on the floor-- on the street.
Now the officer didn't know what to do, our crew.
To send more through?
He just couldn't believe it what happened.
And-- but all of a sudden, one-- we had a guy who was our-- not-- he was not in charge, but he was our leader.
We recognized him as a leader.
And his name was-- was [?
Pishto, ?] big guy.
We called him the big guy, [?
Poli. ?] [INAUDIBLE] [?
Poli. ?] That was the big guy in Hungarian.
And he says, we are going in.
But he first told us we drop everything on the street, what we have.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All-- all the valuables we had to throw away on the street.
And the only thing which-- which I left for myself was the-- the postcard which I kept-- [SOBBING] I kept in my hand, my fist.
I did not throw away.
I kept my shoes.
I kept my underwear.
And maybe I kept a sweater too.
I don't remember.
So when-- when-- but my turn didn't come yet, because before there was-- there were other boys.
So the guy who was religious, the religious guy I mentioned, he put on quickly this [NON-ENGLISH] this kind of a-- how do you call it-- the [NON-ENGLISH], when they pray in the morning.
And he put on-- on his head, the tallit and he start-- start praying.
So we all throw on the street.
Kids come around and they grabbed everything from the street and they run away with all the belongings which we had and bring it to them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we moved.
Our turn came.
And the other guys didn't come out already to us.
Because they kept them in the backyard, so the people on the seat wouldn't see in what shape-- so we shouldn't see in what shape they are coming out.
See?
So three, three, three, went in, went in, till my turn came.
So I went in with-- with Benjamin-- Benjamin and [?
Loti, ?] my friends.
And we walked in-- the [INAUDIBLE] was-- the walkway inside the building.
But the entrance was inside, in the backyard.
So we came to a big-- to a big door-- a big gate.
Open.
The gate was open, the door.
And you saw a lobby inside, a nice, beautiful building.
And we went inside.
We saw nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

But as we walked inside the lobby, towards the staircase, to go up the floor, from behind these two big doors jumped out two-- jumped two big Nilosh in black uniform, pick up them whips-- large whips-- and-- and-- and start to hitting and-- and beating us up.
So we start to run up the staircase fast they shouldn't be able to hit us, but he was running after us up to the top floor.
And there we-- we run inside a big hall.
We saw behind a big desk, one Gestapo in a white-- in a white-- A shirt?
In a white-- no.
In a white-- like-- like a doctor, you know?
A white-- yes.
A white coat.
So-- a robe, a white robe, looked like a doctor.
And on the floor, there were things laying [INAUDIBLE].
The shoes were separate.
There was a stack of shoes, stack of underwear, and stack of pants and-- and jackets.
So when we came in, he said right away to undress, everything undress, and put there on the stair.
So we put everything there.
And then came out, these two Nilosh which they were-- were chasing us up the floor, the two.
Another two were in there, inside.
And-- no, another one inside.
And each of them grabbed us by the hair like that.
I had hair short like this.
And started to pull our hair out and all kind of-- each of them grabbed the hair of somebody-- of one of the guys.
And the doctor said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] name?
Each of us has to give the name.
Name, I gave my name.
He give the name.
Third give the name.
And then he said, where are you from?
I said, [HUNGARIAN].
In Hungarian, [HUNGARIAN] is [HUNGARIAN].
When I said, [HUNGARIAN] and he was holding me by the head, he give me a punch me up, that I was-- my head was swinging around.
You are from-- listen!
He was from [HUNGARIAN], he says.
You are from [HUNGARIAN]?
Yes.
Another punch.
And he kicked and punched in the stomach, and in my face, all over.
And he says, what they're doing for a living?
[HUNGARIAN] means in Hungarian, is a sign-- sign painter.
Oh, listen to this.
He is from [HUNGARIAN] and he is a [HUNGARIAN].
So he kept hitting me and kicking me, that I already was numb.
I didn't even feel-- could feel-- I couldn't even feel anymore the pain which he-- before I was felt.
I was so numb, that I found myself lying on the floor.
And he tried to revive me with water-- cold water.
And it didn't help.
So he-- he took a cigarette, put-- lit a cigarette and he tried, and put me here on the-- on the cigarette, on my body, tried to revive.
So this, I was-- I jumped up from pain.
And this was-- happened also to my friends.
And this was-- then he said, get dressed.
He throw me some kind of a rags over there, which I couldn't even put them on.
And I came out naked with the rags in my hand, with the shoes in my hand.
And this I would get to the backyard, between all of the guys who were there.
And we tried to put them on, little by little.
And I managed to put on the shoes, and to put on my pants.
I had no-- no buttons.
With some kind of a string, I had to fix it.
And the jacket also has no buttons.
It was small.
The-- the shoe was small.
I couldn't put in no-- nothing.
I put in paper so I couldn't feel the cold.
I put some paper on my-- my feet.
And we were waiting there in this backyard till all the guys get through this inspection.
Then it was already dark-- it got already dark, evening.
And he came down-- the head of this Nilosh-- Nilosh came down.
And he said, attention.
Attention, everybody.
Who came down from this office of mine?
And he said to his fellow man that the Russians are coming, and they will pay for it.
We will pay for it, he says.
Who was the guy who said this?
Nobody answered.
Nobody said anything.
OK, he says.
I give you 10 minutes. 10 minutes I give you.
If nobody came out, then I will put you in a line, line up, and every 10th person will have to step out and I will take them away.
He will not come anymore back.
So we were lined up, and he says 10 minutes.
And we were standing and waiting.
We were-- everybody was looking.
You shouldn't be the 10th person in this line.
See, I was afraid.
If I would be the 10th, and started to, you know, to maneuver back and forth.
And now the 10 pers-- the 10 minutes are over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10 minutes are over, yeah, and this-- we-- I was standing next to this [? menchel, ?] to this-- to this religious guy.
And he says something that I don't know, about, God, about this, all kind of things.
And 10 minutes are over and he was-- didn't finish his prayer, this [? menchel, ?] when the officer said, OK, 10 minutes are over.
And one guy came out of the-- of the line.
It was this large [?
Poli, ?] the-- the-- the-- our leader.
Pishto, the large [?
Poli, ?] the leader came out and says, hold it.
I'm the one who said this.
But it was not-- we know it's not true, because he just wanted to-- to save from us this trouble of the 10th per-- people to get killed.
So he said, he stepped out, and then the [?
Nilosh ?] came with a gun, like this.
And he says, move it, and they took him away.
He was waving like this, I remember.
But we never saw him back.
So we were there, in this backyard, after that.
And they told us to line up again, and we are going and moving away.
So we went.
It was night and cold and it started to rain and-- with snow together like now, this kind of a weather-- sleet and [INAUDIBLE].
And we went in this rain, get wet and cold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Don't ask.
And we were going.
So we went for a while, for about 15, 20 minutes.
And they took us inside in the side street.
There was a-- some kind of a farm was there.
It was on the outskirts of the city was it.
Some farm.
And we-- we had to go inside.
In the ground was an opening, like-- like a-- like a-- like a--
Like a bunker.
Like a cellar.
Yes.
But for the summer, the-- the farmer would keep their potatoes stored, you know-- storage thing inside.
But there was no floor there.
It was wet and mud, and was dripping from-- from the floor and cold.
And the potato was taken out.
We found a few potatoes in-- on the floor.
And this small-- the whole-- the thing was so big like this room.
And 200 guys were-- were stuffed in-- inside into this place, sitting in the mud, you know, and dripping on-- on us the whole night.
We couldn't even-- not sleep.
We couldn't.
We were shaking the whole night.
I don't know if I dozed off or not, I don't know.
But it was dawn.
In the morning it started to get daylight.
They chased us out, told us to move it, to move it.
So we were in such a shape at that time, I'll tell you that I don't remember in my life that I ever went through such a thing.
OK.
We move, we walk, and we have to move-- walk-- again, to march again.
We were matching at-- for about two hours.
And also people collapsed on the way-- on the [INAUDIBLE].
But at this time, when I noticed somebody collapsed, there was-- I don't remember if it was a truck or-- or-- or a-- or a horse, or something, horse buggy, or something.
They picked him up and they put them inside.
Didn't leave him there on the floor, on the ground.
But we-- we managed to get to a road-- to an area where there is no-- no-- nothing was there, just an emp-- just a road, you know?
All of a sudden, from a distance, we saw small huts, like small huts, small shacks with-- with smoke coming out, electric wire, around and around, around this whole area.
And there are people who are-- who are shoveling the snow away.
And they told us to sit down there on the snow.
And each three people to go inside again to register.
So we went-- we went.
We didn't even think about it, what is going to happen.
We went inside.
We registered the name.
And-- and they didn't speak to us anything.
They didn't ask us, only the name, the year I was born, where I'm from, that's it.
And he didn't say where it was or nothing.
We didn't know at that time where it was.
We saw it-- it's in Germany, because he speaks German.
We saw when we passed the border between Hungary and Germany, it is in German.
All the streets-- street [INAUDIBLE] plates are written in German, right, on the-- on the roads.
So we know we were in Germany.
But exactly-- it was a name of a-- a name was there, but I don't remember exactly the name.
So I registered.
They-- we standing there and waiting outside again.
It was snowing terribly.
And then they said, OK, let's go.
He took us to a pla-- to a barn which is-- the barn was outside of the camp actually, not inside of the camp.
But then they took again this barn around with-- with wire, with-- with a-- with a fence so we couldn't go away.
They fenced us in.
They made us to work and-- and-- and make for ourselves bunk beds.
They build us all kind of this.
And we ourselves, we got nails and hammers and we started to work and do this bunk beds.
And each one get a blanket, and also a dish-- a metal dish.
We should go and get-- [INAUDIBLE] they would bring to us this big pot, and they would give us soup to eat.
And tomorrow morning, we going to work.
So next morning, they took us to work in a-- in a-- out of this-- in the village, there was a wooden area.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was wood.
Wooden area.
And there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- we were-- we were cutting the wood, this-- the trees.
We had to carry these trees from the hills down to the road, where a truck would come and pick it up.
This was-- lasted a few weeks.
And then they put us to work in the trenches, to dig with a shovel, deep in the trenches.
And this was in such a way that the tank would come down in an angle, right?
And from the other side, he couldn't-- he couldn't come out.
He would get stuck in these trenches.
A deep trench, but with a-- with a triangle, like a triangle way.
This was-- it was done in the whole-- the whole area, the whole area, miles, and miles, and miles.
We were working on that-- on that job.
We were happy to do it, because it was so cold.
We were not dressed, and this way we got warmed up.
So we didn't feel the frost and the-- and the-- the cold so much.
And in the morning, we got soup, the-- also the same thing, coffee or a tea and a slice of bread.
For lunch we got a soup with a slice of bread and a piece of margarine-- a small piece.
For dinner, we got-- we got again a soup, a slice of bread, and a piece of salami.
So we had some kind of a-- we figured how many calories it is, approximately the sa-- the calorie we could make it.
And we were still young so we-- we could overcome this hard work and survive.
But then, one day, I fell when I was working.
During the work I had some-- some problem.
I felt pain in my-- under my belly.
It was that I hadn't had-- it was-- I felt it was a hernia or something.
It bothered me.
So I told my pals about it, what to do.
And they said, don't do it, because those-- he spoke to some-- the guy who went to-- to get the-- the-- the pot with-- with soup from the kitchen.
He had to go-- he is the only one who has contact with other-- with other prisoners.
So from them we find out that this is a military prisoner of war camp in which the prisoners are either Russians, French, or British.
A few British too, but they're mostly French.
So he said, when he-- when he was asking, where are we now?
He said we are satellite stalag-- satellite stalag camp of prisoners, which we get all the provisions from Mauthausen, not far from here.
It's a concentration camp.
And if something is-- happens, they took us there.
So we know that we are not far from-- from Mauthausen.
And the next cities in the-- in the area were called Fellbach and there was Linz.
Linz is one city, and Fellbach.
Between Linz and Fellbach is located the concentration camp, Mauthausen.
And we were one of the satellites working for this proj-- job.
And this we knew.
So he came back with the-- with this guy.
And I told this-- my friend, listen what happens.
I feel this pain.
He says, don't report this.
You have to do, because otherwise they take you Mauthausen.
So I couldn't.
I-- I-- I-- one day, I collapsed during the work-- during my work.
And-- and the guard, the SS guard who were guarding us on the job happened to be not Germans, but Croatians.
These Croatians were now in Croatia-- and were fighting this in [?
Sincero ?] in Bosnia.
You know Croatians?
These also-- I have to mention it, because these also-- the Croatians, when Germany invaded Yugoslavia, the Serbs and the Jews were persecuted.
The Croatians were the only people who the Germans give them-- give them their own country, independence, because they had a-- a Nazi party called Ustashi.
And this Ustashi, they volunteered for SS, for this job, and they happened to be in our-- in our-- where I was working there, on this job, this Ustashi.
And he talked to me in-- in-- not in German.
He didn't even know German to speak.
He asked me where I am from.
I says, I'm from Czechoslovakia.
And he say, he start to speak to me in Croatian.
But this language is very con-- very similar.
Similar.
So he said, I'll take care of you.
And he says, you come with me.
Instead of taking me to-- to Mauthausen, he took another two French [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prisoners also with him.
He took us on the train station, and I didn't know where we are going.
He said, come with me.
Don't worry about it, he says.
So I was happy that he speaks with me-- spoke-- he understand my language.
I spoke half Russian, half Czech.
Half with my hands.
We could communicate.
He understood my language.
And I saw that we are moving towards-- not towards Linz and Fellbach inside Germany.
We're moving out in the other direction, toward the Hungarian border.
And we crossed Hungarian border.
The train took us to a station where there was a large town.
And says, here's this-- there's a hospital in this town, and you come with me, he told me.
But in the train, it was a story.
He said in the train, I was sitting with them.
I had this yellow band and the train was-- was crowded.
And one guy was drunk or something.
He came over and he says he wants to sit down where I'm sitting.
And this guy, he said it in Hungarian.
So this-- the-- the guard who don't speak Hungarian, he's asking me, what is he saying?
I said, this guy says, in Hungarian, he wants to punch me up.
He wants to punch me up.
And now he says, no, no, no.
The-- the-- the Ustashi guy makes with the finger, no, no, no.
Like saying, he won't let him to sit down where a Jew is sitting.
And he goes like this, he says, [INAUDIBLE], he start cursing.
This guy is not a German, he is a Russian.
And he is a fake guy.
And he's now holding a Jew.
And he won't let me sit down in his place.
And he start cursing.
Don't ask me.
He made such a fuss in this whole train, that other people in the train started to-- to tell him, stop it!
Stop it!
And there were some who were coming with him and saying, he is right.
And he came also to-- to-- to-- to discuss and to-- and to the-- what is going on here?
Debating the whole thing, and cursing and [INAUDIBLE].
Don't ask.
It was a big hassle there.
Finally, we got-- in the meantime, we got to Budapest.
I was sitting there.
He wouldn't let him to-- to sit even on my place.
This-- because I told them this in-- in his language, about what he meant.
Then we get down.
We get off the train.
Again?
